segments.title: Reimagining Dreadnought with triple 14-inch turrets
segments.summary: A game design exercise asks what HMS Dreadnought might look like with triple 14-inch turrets, more speed, and a deliberately modernised secondary battery.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the ship designer to test an intentionally ahistorical Dreadnought: 14-inch guns in triple turrets and a 24- to 25-knot hull. The moment usefully separates a visually appealing layout from the weight, funnel, range, and secondary-armament compromises needed to make it work. He also contrasts long-range main-battery fire with the much shorter reach of six- and three-inch weapons.

evidence.note: He designs a faster Dreadnought with triple 14-inch turrets and discusses its guns, funnels, weight, and layout.

segments.title: Repairing a Spanish dreadnought design
segments.summary: A flawed game-generated Spanish capital ship becomes a practical discussion of balanced guns, armour, machinery, and displacement.
segments.body: The starting design combines awkward armour choices, inefficient machinery, and a confused secondary battery. Dr. Clarke replaces or rationalises its weapons and protection while keeping an eye on speed and displacement, then notes that a 60,000-ton Spanish ship in 1920 would itself be an extraordinary industrial proposition. It is a compact illustration of why a paper battleship is a system of trade-offs rather than a list of maximum values.

evidence.note: He corrects the Spanish design's gun, armour, machinery, speed, and displacement choices.

segments.title: Firepower, numbers, and effective range
segments.summary: The first simulated battle shows why a broadside and numerical advantage can overwhelm individually formidable ships before close-range weapons matter.
segments.body: The British side fields sixty 14-inch guns against a smaller Spanish force with 15-inch weapons, making the exercise a comparison of salvo weight, numbers, and engagement distance. Dr. Clarke treats the three-inch battery as anti-destroyer protection rather than a weapon for duelling capital ships. He also cautions that the game’s firing arcs and hit behaviour can produce implausible results, especially when ships appear to fire while badly angled or into the water.

evidence.note: The battle comparison covers 15- versus 14-inch guns, numerical superiority, and the intended role of three-inch weapons.

segments.title: Why dreadnoughts should not close like pre-dreadnoughts
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke criticises the simulation’s tendency to close the range when the ships were designed to exploit long-range heavy-gun fire.
segments.body: The game’s AI repeatedly pulls capital ships toward six-inch range even though their principal advantage is long-range 18-inch gunnery. Dr. Clarke frames this as a mismatch between design and tactics: dreadnought-era fleets should use range, fire control, and armour to fight at distance rather than behave like earlier close-range ships. At very short range, he argues, no plausible amount of armour makes a ship safe from repeated heavy-shell hits.

evidence.note: He contrasts long-range dreadnought fighting with the AI's repeated decision to close into secondary-gun range.

segments.title: The Colossus and the limits of maximum battleship design
segments.summary: A deliberately absurd 400,000-ton design tests how armour, huge guns, torpedoes, and machinery interact when practical limits are ignored.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pushes the game’s options toward an almost unsinkable battleship, adding extreme armour, bulkheads, large-calibre guns, torpedoes, radar, and the machinery needed to keep the hull moving. The thought experiment is not a practical proposal; it exposes how quickly the requirements for protection, armament, range, and propulsion drive displacement upward. He turns the result into an alternate-history question about the shock such a ship would cause if it appeared in the First World War.

evidence.note: He designs the 400,000-ton Colossus and discusses its armour, 20.9-inch guns, torpedoes, radar, and First World War implications.

segments.title: Building an Italian 1920 battleship
segments.summary: A second design pass explores an Italian-style 1920 battleship through belt layout, hull form, secondary guns, ammunition, and weight balance.
segments.body: Working from a 1920 Italian premise, Dr. Clarke experiments with a double hull, heavy belt protection, geared turbines, 15-inch guns, and six-inch secondary armament. The discussion repeatedly returns to the practical penalty of each choice: more armour and guns can create a weight-offset or displacement problem that has to be solved elsewhere. He also objects when generic game components make Italian designs look German, because national design traditions are not interchangeable.

evidence.note: He develops an Italian 1920 design, discussing armour, hull form, turbines, 15-inch guns, six-inch secondaries, and weight.

segments.title: Torpedo range, fire control, and ammunition endurance
segments.summary: The Italian-Spanish trial becomes a discussion of fighting around torpedo range, local control, secondary hits, and ammunition capacity.
segments.body: At roughly nine to ten kilometres, the ships are inside both torpedo and six-inch-gun range, a distance Dr. Clarke considers exceptionally close by Jutland standards. He argues that fire-control conditions should make hits much more likely than the simulation suggests, and observes that eight-inch hits are doing more visible damage than the heavy guns. The prolonged engagement also illustrates why ammunition stowage matters: a force that runs short cannot simply rely on nominal gun calibre or protection.

evidence.note: He discusses nine-kilometre torpedo and six-inch range, local control, eight-inch damage, and ammunition endurance.

segments.title: Could Poland's Grom serve as an export Tribal?
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether the Polish Grom class resembles the Royal Navy's desired fleet destroyer, prompting a comparison with the Tribal class.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke says the comparison is close, though not exact: the Grom is somewhat heavier and has different gun and torpedo arrangements. Its general-purpose balance is the key point. A smaller navy benefits more from a flexible destroyer that can perform several fleet tasks than from a narrowly specialised ship.
segments.question: Was the Polish Grom class close to the destroyer the Royal Navy wanted?
segments.answerShort: Broadly yes: it was close to an export Tribal in size and general-purpose intent, though its armament details differed from British preferences.

evidence.note: He compares the Grom's dimensions and weapons with Tribal-class and Royal Navy destroyer requirements.

segments.title: Three España-class ships against SMS Kaiser
segments.summary: A what-if question compares three Spanish España-class dreadnoughts with a German Kaiser-class ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects the three Spanish ships to prevail, but not cleanly. The numerical advantage does not erase the danger of fighting a more formidable individual opponent: he expects Spain to suffer losses or crippling damage, with perhaps only one ship remaining immediately serviceable. The answer is a useful warning against treating three-to-one odds as automatic tactical safety.
segments.question: Who would win: three España-class battleships or SMS Kaiser?
segments.answerShort: The Spanish force would probably win, but likely at a severe cost, with multiple España-class ships sunk, crippled, or left unserviceable.

evidence.note: He judges that the Spanish would probably win but suffer serious losses despite their numerical advantage.

segments.title: A Spanish Vittorio Veneto and Mediterranean strategy
segments.summary: An alternate-history question examines how a Spanish battleship comparable to Vittorio Veneto might alter British force allocation before and during the Second World War.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that such a ship would be strategically valuable to Germany if Spain possessed it before the war, forcing Britain to reconsider Mediterranean and Atlantic capital-ship deployments. He expects Force H and the Mediterranean Fleet to receive particular attention, while British procurement might accelerate in response to a more threatening Spanish-Italian combination. Yet he also stresses Spain's post-civil-war weakness, exposed harbours, and thin escort force: submarines, especially T-class boats firing large salvos from ambush, could be a more practical British answer than a symmetrical battleship contest.
segments.question: How would a Spanish Vittorio Veneto-type battleship affect Second World War naval strategy?
segments.answerShort: It could redirect British capital ships and procurement, but Spain's weak escorts and harbour defence would leave the battleship vulnerable to submarine ambush.

evidence.note: He considers Spanish alignment, British fleet allocation, submarine ambush, post-civil-war weakness, and Anglo-Spanish industrial links.
